coredata.dc:title: Dual-channel intelligent logic gate combined with smartphone RGB for detecting glutathione based on ultra-sensitive ratio fluorescence sensing platform using food waste carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Arkin, Kamile
Zheng, Yuxin
Ma, Xuesong
Xu, Yuting
Jiang, Xue
Shang, Qingkun
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) plays a crucial role in cellular redox balance, protection of hemoglobin in red blood cells, and immune regulation by scavenging free radicals and enhancing antioxidant enzyme activity. Therefore, developing fast, sensitive, and portable methods for GSH detection has great application value in medical research. Building ultra-sensitive, visual, and portable fluorescent probes has always been a hot research topic for carbon dots (CDs). In this paper, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) with blue fluorescence were prepared from inedible fiber in jackfruit and doped with N-acetyl-L-cysteine. Based on a thorough investigation of the morphology and optical properties of the NS-CDs, a high-sensitivity ratio fluorescence sensing platform for glutathione (GSH) detection was successfully developed by utilizing the interaction between the o-phenylenediamine (OPD), Ag+, NS-CDs, and GSH. Further, a dual-channel intelligent logic gate and a portable, visual GSH detection method using a smartphone RGB application were designed. The detection limit of GSH is 9.36 nmol/L. Due to the significant change in color from yellow to light pink and then to blue on the sensing platform, it is visible to the naked eye and makes it easier for smartphones to recognize colors. The combination of dual-channel intelligent logic gates and smartphone RGB methods further ensures accurate detection of GSH. The research work in this article not only developed a novel, visual, and ultra-sensitive ratio fluorescence method for detecting GSH but also provided new ideas for promoting the reuse of food waste.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratio fluorescence sensing platform
Dual-channel intelligent logic gate
Smartphone RGB
Carbon dots
Glutathione

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The optimal composition and experimental conditions for probes
Detection method using ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Fluorescent property of CDs and NS-CDs
Construction of a sensing platform for detection of GSH based on NS-CDs
Experimental support for building a sensing platform
Condition and composition optimization of sensor platform
Selectivity and anti-interference ability of sensor platform
Visual determination of GSH using a smartphone
Determination of GSH in human urine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM of CDs (a and b) and NS-CDs (c and d). The XRD spectra (e), FTIR spectra (f), XPS spectra full spectra (g) and N 1s (h) of CDs and NS-CDs, S 2p (i) of NS-CDs.
Three-dimensional excitation-emission map (a and b), UV–vis absorption spectra (c) and FL decay curves (d) of CDs and NS-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra (a-c); UV–vis spectra of OPDox (Yellow line) and fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs (Blue line) (d); Fluorescence decay curves of emission at 450 (e) and 560 nm (f); Basic schematic diagram of the construction of a ratio fluorescence sensing platform (g).

The effect of different volume of NS-CDs on fluorescence spectra of system (NS-CDs + Ag+ + OPD) (a), system (NS-CDs + Ag+ + OPD + GSH) (b), IBlue/IYellow (c) and their corresponding photographs taken under a 390 nm UV lamp (d); Fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs + Ag+ + OPD with varying GSH concentrations (e), standard curve and linear equation (f), CIE color coordinates (g) of a ratio fluorescent platform with GSH concentration range of 500–2500 nmol/L.

Visualization detection of GSH using RGB mode of smartphone, data output, and linear relationship between RGB value and GSH concentration (a), Logic gate circuit diagram (b), histogram of the fluorescence intensity (c) and truth table of the sensor platform(d).
Determination of GSH spiked in real samples.
The comparison of the proposed strategy with previous methods for the detection of GSH.

head.ce:title: Dual-channel intelligent logic gate combined with smartphone RGB for detecting glutathione based on ultra-sensitive ratio fluorescence sensing platform using food waste carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, 130024 Changchun, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, 130024 Changchun, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) plays a crucial role in cellular redox balance, protection of hemoglobin in red blood cells, and immune regulation by scavenging free radicals and enhancing antioxidant enzyme activity. Therefore, developing fast, sensitive, and portable methods for GSH detection has great application value in medical research. Building ultra-sensitive, visual, and portable fluorescent probes has always been a hot research topic for carbon dots (CDs). In this paper, nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) with blue fluorescence were prepared from inedible fiber in jackfruit and doped with N-acetyl-L-cysteine. Based on a thorough investigation of the morphology and optical properties of the NS-CDs, a high-sensitivity ratio fluorescence sensing platform for glutathione (GSH) detection was successfully developed by utilizing the interaction between the o-phenylenediamine (OPD), Ag+, NS-CDs, and GSH. Further, a dual-channel intelligent logic gate and a portable, visual GSH detection method using a smartphone RGB application were designed. The detection limit of GSH is 9.36 nmol/L. Due to the significant change in color from yellow to light pink and then to blue on the sensing platform, it is visible to the naked eye and makes it easier for smartphones to recognize colors. The combination of dual-channel intelligent logic gates and smartphone RGB methods further ensures accurate detection of GSH. The research work in this article not only developed a novel, visual, and ultra-sensitive ratio fluorescence method for detecting GSH but also provided new ideas for promoting the reuse of food waste.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of carbon dots (CDs) with excellent fluorescence properties from food waste is not only environmentally friendly but also cost-effective. It has attracted widespread attention in detecting metal ions [1], drug small molecules [2], and environmental pollutants [3,4]. However, the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon dots prepared from food waste is usually low. The detection method established solely based on quenching or enhancement of carbon dots has low sensitivity and poor anti-interference ability. These two issues seriously constrain the practical application of carbon dots.

To improve the detection sensitivity of carbon dot fluorescent probes, the first problem to be solved is to increase the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon dots. Doping nitrogen or sulfur elements or surface during CDs’ preparation, or functionalization of carbon dots can effectively regulate their optical properties enhance fluorescence quantum yield [5]. Our research group synthesized CDs using food waste such as cherry tomato stems [6] and broccoli stems [7]. The introduction of amino acids and polyethyleneimine significantly improved the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs during the synthesis process, which was successfully applied to the visual detection of tetracycline antibiotics and the visual adsorption of Pb2+ in wastewater.

Constructing ratio fluorescent probes is an excellent strategy to simultaneously improve the detection sensitivity and anti-interference ability of carbon dot fluorescent probes [8]. Ratio fluorescence sensors emit fluorescence at two distinct wavelengths: one serves as a self-calibration signal, while the other functions as the detection signal. This dual-emission mechanism minimizes interference from external factors and enhances their applicability to complex biological or environmental systems. Therefore, it can effectively avoid false positive/false negative errors caused by one signal output of fluorescence sensors [9,10].

Ratio fluorescence sensors have gained traction in various fields, owing to their precision and adaptability [11,12]. For example, Lin et al. [13] synthesized red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) from glutathione and formamide as raw materials and used them as optical probes to construct a homemade low-cost sensing platform that can dynamically monitor the concentration of Cu2+ in different plant species. The detection limit of R-CDs for Cu2+ in water samples is 0.375 nmol/L and 11.7 mg/kg in plants. In addition, the sensing platform can be used to report the distribution of Cu2+ in the external environment of plants, proving its suitability for monitoring Cu2+ in living plants and the surrounding environment.

Glutathione (GSH) plays a crucial role in cellular redox balance, protection of hemoglobin in red blood cells, and immune regulation by scavenging free radicals and enhancing antioxidant enzyme activity [14,15]. Abnormal GSH levels can indicate imbalances in the antioxidant system or health issues, making GSH detection important for evaluating health status [16]. Current GSH detection methods include liquid chromatography [17], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [18], electrochemical methods [19], fluorescence spectroscopy [20], and colorimetric analysis [21,22]. Despite their effectiveness, these methods often rely on bulky instrumentation and labor-intensive sample preparation, limiting their practicality for rapid or field-based applications. Therefore, the development of fast, sensitive, and portable GSH detection methods holds significant promise for advancing medical research. In recent years, the detection of GSH based on fluorescence sensing technology has made great progress. For example, Wang et al. [23] synthesized self-assembled copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) that leverage a turn-on fluorescence mechanism for GSH detection in neutral and alkaline environments, achieving a detection range of 1–100 µmol/L and a detection limit of 300 nmol/L. Similarly, Liu et al. [24] developed a quantum dot-based “off–on” lateral flow biosensor capable of detecting GSH with a limit of detection (LOD) of 25 nmol/L and high recovery rates in HeLa cell extracts. Another approach by Wang et al. [25] utilized MnO2 nanosheet-CuNC composites to achieve an LOD of 100 nmol/L, demonstrating strong selectivity against other biothiols and metal ions. Furthermore, Ji et al. [26] reported a highly selective GSH detection strategy based on a G-rich oligonucleotide forming a fluorescent G-quadruplex structure in the presence of Ag(I) ions. Upon the introduction of GSH, the G-quadruplex is disrupted due to GSH's strong affinity for Ag(I), resulting in fluorescence quenching. This method achieved an exceptional LOD of 3.5 nmol/L, making it effective even in complex biological samples such as cell extracts.

In this work, the fibrous inedible part of jackfruit pulp was used as the carbon source, and N-acetyl-L-cysteine was dopped to synthesize carbon dots (NS-CDs) by hydrothermal method. NS-CDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence at 450 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield of 11.8 %. Since GSH does not affect the fluorescence emission of NS-CDs, it cannot be directly detected using NS-CDs. Therefore, a dual emission ratio fluorescence sensing platform for detecting GSH was constructed by cleverly utilizing the interaction between NS-CDs, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), Ag+, and glutathione. Based on the dual emission fluorescence intensity ratio (IBlue/IYellow), a dual-channel intelligent logic gate and a portable RGB detection method for GSH using smartphones were designed, and the GSH content in human urine was accurately measured. Since de Silva et al. proposed the use of AND logic gates to process light signals in 1993, fluorescent molecular logic gates have attracted widespread attention in analytical chemistry [27,28]. We designed a dual-channel intelligent logic gate with NS-CDs and Ag++OPD, and GSH as input signals, and yellow, white, and blue fluorescence as output signals. The judgment results of the application logic gate can be mutually verified with the results obtained by applying the smartphone RGB method, ensuring the accuracy of the measurement results. This study not only expands the application of CDs in the medical field, but also proposes new strategies for the high value-added reuse of food waste.

The biomass raw material jackfruit shreds (the fibrous substance between the pulp of jackfruit, usually inedible) was sourced from a city supermarket (Changchun City, China). N-acetyl-L-cysteine, glutathione (GSH), tryptophan (Trp), alanine (Ala), methionine (Met), proline (Pro), phenylalanine (Phe), serine (Ser), valine (Val), uric acid (UA), and Urea were all purchased from Macklin Company in Shanghai, China. o-phenylenediamine (OPD), ZnCl2, MnCl2, MgCl2, KCl, NaCl, CaCl2, CrCl3, CdCl2, NaAc, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, Na2SO4, NaOH, HCl, and AgNO3 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Factory, China. All these chemicals are analytically pure. Ultra-pure water used in the experiments was filtered through a Millipore ultra-pure water system (USA). The raw materials were freeze-dried using a lyophilizer (Alpha 1-4 LD plus, Germany).

Transmission electron microscopy images of CDs were obtained by transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100 PLUS, Japan; Test conditions: accelerated voltage 200 KV, point resolution 0.19 nm, line resolution 0.1 nm). A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, USA) was used to test the infrared absorption spectrum of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) testing was performed by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs were obtained by Smartlab X-ray powder diffractometer. The characteristic absorption of the samples was analyzed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Thermo Genesys 10S, USA; Test conditions: Spectral range 200–600 nm). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-320, China; integration time 1 s, scanning speed 1200 nm/min, slit width 10 nm, cuvette path length 1 cm). Absolute fluorescence quantum yield and time-resolved fluorescence decay data of the samples were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, UK). Quantification of GSH in actual samples was performed by high-performance liquid chromatography and evaporative light detector (HPLC, Agilent 1200).

Synthesis of CDs: 0.5 g jackfruit shreds of freeze-dried powder were added to 10 mL deionized water. The mixed solution was sonicated for 10 min and then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon autoclave liner reactor, heated at 200 °C for 8 h. Subsequently, the solutions obtained were first centrifuged to remove unreacted impurities and then filtered with 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried for subsequent characterization and applications.

Synthesis of NS-CDs: 0.5 g jackfruit shreds of freeze-dried powder and 0.15 g N-acetyl-L-cysteine were added to 10 mL deionized water. The mixed solution was sonicated for 10 min and then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon autoclave liner reactor and heated for 8 h at 200 °C. Subsequently, the solutions obtained were first centrifuged to remove unreacted impurities and then filted with 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried for subsequent characterization and applications.
For comparison, we synthesized carbon dots using N-acetyl-L-cysteine as the raw material according to the above method, denoted as NL-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimal composition and experimental conditions for probes
ce:section.ce:para: Measure the emission spectra of NS CDs and OPDox at different excitation wavelengths, and determine the optimal excitation wavelength based on the difference in their fluorescence emission intensity.

The following conditions remain unchanged: excitation wavelength of 390 nm, 180 µL Ag + solution (6 mmol/L), pH = 7.0 buffer solution, and incubation at 30 °C for 10 min. To investigate the effect of adding different volumes (0–90 µL) of OPD solution (6 mol/L) on the fluorescence intensity of OPDox and determine the optimal amount of OPD added.

Similarly, under the determined optimal conditions, the effects of different pH values (5.5–8.0), incubation temperatures (20–60 °C) and times (1–30 min), and NS CDs addition amounts (0–100 µL) on the fluorescence intensity of OPDox were investigated to determine the optimal formation conditions and composition of the probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection method using ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: 100 µL of sample with different concentrations of GSH solution was mixed with 180 µL (6 mmol/L) AgNO3 solution, 60 µL (6 mmol/L) OPD, and 60 µL (1.35 mg/mL) NS-CDs solution. The pH of the system was adjusted to 7.5 using a buffer solution. The mixture was vibrated and incubated for 10 min at 40 °C in a water bath. The emission spectra under 398 nm excitation were recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer.

All experiments were performed in accordance with the Guidelines of Northeast Normal University and approved by the Ethics Committee of Northeast Normal University. All participants provided informed consent prior to their inclusion in the study, in accordance with the ethical standards of the institutional and/or national research committee and with the 1964 Helsinki declaration and its later amendments or comparable ethical standards.

The urine samples of two healthy young women were centrifuged, filtered, and diluted 100 times. Take two 100 μL urine samples, add an appropriate amount of glutathione standard solution to one of them, and add an equal amount of distilled water to the other. Measure the concentration of GSH using the ratio fluorescence sensor and smartphone RGB constructed in this article, and HPLC methods, respectively. The HPLC conditions were as follows: the mobile phase consisted of acetonitrile and 0.1 % formic acid at a ratio of 10:90, with a flow rate of 0.8 mL/min, and an evaporative light-scattering detector set to 111 °C. Measure five times in parallel and calculate the standard deviation.

The size and morphology of two types of CDs were observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The TEM image (Fig. 1
a) shows that CDs are uniformly and monodisperse nanodot structures, with an average size of approximately 3.04 nm. HRTEM images and Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) models (Fig. 1 b) show that CDs exhibit clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm. Fig. 1 c and d show the TEM and HRTEM images of NS-CDs, with an average size of 3.57 nm and a lattice stripe spacing of 0.34 nm. These indicate that the particle size and lattice of CDs undergo changes after N and S doping. The broad peak at 21.3° in the X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) of two types of CDs (Fig. 1 e) corresponds to the graphite structure, confirming the successful synthesis of CDs.

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1 f) shows that the two CDs have similar vibrational rotational absorption characteristics. For example, the broad absorption peak at 3000–3500 cm−1 is the stretching vibration peak of O–H or the N–H of amine, indicating the presence of amino and hydroxyl groups. The peaks at 1600, 1400, and 1100 cm−1 correspond to C = O, C–N, and C–O/C–S [29], respectively. The intensity of C = O, C–N or N–H stretching vibration absorption of NS-CDs located at 1590 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 or 3150–3410 cm−1, respectively, are significantly higher than that of CDs, which may be due to an increased nitrogen content derived from N-acetyl-L-cysteine during synthesis.

The XPS spectra of CDs and NS-CDs (Fig. 1 g) show that the main elemental components of CDs are C, N, and O, while NS-CDs also contain S element, and the peak intensity of N 1s increases. From the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1 h), the peaks at 398.8, 399.6, and 401.4 eV correspond to C = N, N–H, and C–N–C bonds [30]. Moreover, the content of C–N–C in NS-CDs is higher than that in CDs. Fig. 1 i further confirms the presence of the S element, showing four different peaks at binding energies of 163.2 and 164.4 eV in the S 2p spectrum, corresponding to C–S–C and S–C bonds [31]. Fig. S1a and b list the high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs and NS-CDs. In the C 1s spectrum, the three peaks at 284.3, 285.5, and 287.6 eV belong to C = C/C–C, C–O/C–N, and C = O bonds, respectively. In the O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1b), two signal peaks at 530.8 and 532.0 eV correspond to C = O bonds and C–O bonds, respectively. The elemental contents obtained from XPS measurements are listed in Fig. S1c. The nitrogen content of NS-CDs increases from 2.97 % to 4.63 %, the N/C ratio increases from 0.045 to 0.068. This indicates that the doping of N-acetyl-L-cysteine has a significant impact on the composition and structure of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent property of CDs and NS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of CDs and NS-CDs are shown in Fig. 2
a and b. The optimal excitation wavelengths of CDs and NS-CDs are all 370 nm while the optimal emission wavelengths are also close to each other, at 438 nm and 436 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 c, compared with CDs, the absorption spectrum of NS-CDs shows an overall red-shift, and the absorption peak at 205 nm and shoulder peaks at 245, 270, and 313 nm are more pronounced. This indicates that the introduction of N-acetyl-L-cysteine enhances π–π and n–π transitions and conjugated structural features. The absolute QY of CDs and NS-CDs were determined to be 6.11 % and 11.80 %, respectively, with the latter being 1.93 times higher than the former. The fluorescence decay curves of CDs and NS-CDs (Fig. 2 d) follow a double exponential formula, with the average lifetimes of CDs and NS-CDs being approximately 3.41 and 4.43 ns, respectively. The average lifetime of NS-CDs is significantly higher than that of CDs. According to Eqs. (1) and (2), the radiative rate constant Kr and non-radiative rate constant Knr of CDs and NS-CDs were calculated (Wan et al., 2023) and shown in Table S1. The Kr of CDs (0.0179) was less than that of NS CDs (0.0266), while the Knr (0.275) was greater than that of NS-CDs (0.199). The Knr of CDs is 15.4 times its Kr, while the Knr of NS-CDs is only 7.5 times its Kr. This indicates that the nonradiative transition is dominant in CDs. In contrast, the proportion of non-radiative transitions in NS-CDs is significantly reduced, which may be the main reason for the increase of fluorescence QY [32,33].
(1)
K r
= Q Y / τ
(2)
K
nr
=
( 1 - Q Y )
/ τ

To confirm the effect of N-acetyl-L-cysteine on NS-CDs, we investigated the spectral characteristics of NL-CDs prepared with N-acetyl-L-cysteine as a carbon source under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. S2a, NL-CDs have three absorption peaks at 233, 260, and 305 nm, respectively. While the overall absorption profile is similar to that of NS-CDs, subtle differences in peak positions and the presence of shoulder peaks indicate slight structural or electronic variations, likely due to modifications in surface states or bonding environments introduced by the doped elements. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are 350 and 430 nm as shown in its three-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Fig. S2b), which are also different from those of CDs or NS-CDs. The fluorescence spectra of CDs, NL-CDs, and NS-CDs excited at different wavelengths are shown in Fig. S3. The properties of CDs and NS-CDs are similar. As the excitation wavelength increases, the changing trend of emission wavelengths is the same, but the emission intensity of NS-CDs is significantly enhanced. The emission wavelength of NL-CDs varies with the excitation wavelength, and the emission intensity is much lower than that of NS-CDs. This indicates that the introduction of N-Acetyl-L-Cysteine has a significant impact on the fluorescence emission intensity of NS-CDs.

Carbon dots are often the core components of sensors, and their stability directly affects the application. The fluorescence characteristics of CDs and NS-CDs under different pH conditions are shown in Fig. S4. The fluorescence intensity both of CDs and NS-CDs is unchanged in the pH range of 3–9, while decreases under strong acid and base conditions (pH = 1, 2 and pH = 11–13). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs is always higher than that of CDs. The effects of ion strength (based on NaCl concentration), ambient temperature, continuous excitation, and long-term storage on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs and NS-CDs are shown in Fig. S5 . When the concentration of NaCl is controlled between 0 and 100 mmol/L, the fluorescence intensity both of CDs and NS-CDs remains unchanged (Fig. S5a). This indicates that an environment with high concentrations of NaCl will not affect the stability of biomass CDs. When the ambient temperature increases from 0 °C to 20 °C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs and NS-CDs only slightly decreases. When the temperature was further raised to 80 °C, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased by 19.6 %, while the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs only decreased by 12.4 % (Fig. S5b). The temperature stability of NS-CDs is superior to that of CDs. When CDs and NS-CDs were continuously irradiated under ultraviolet light for 60 min, the fluorescence intensity of CDs and NS-CDs decreased by 19.1 % and 16.0 %, respectively (Fig. S5c). After being stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for one month, the fluorescence intensity of CDs and NS-CDs decreased by 18.3 % and 13.1 %, respectively (Fig. S5d). The pH, ion strength, temperature, anti-photobleaching, and time stability of NS-CDs modified by N-Acetyl-L-Cysteine doping are better than CDs directly derived from biomass. This lays the foundation for its in-depth application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a sensing platform for detection of GSH based on NS-CDs
Experimental support for building a sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, the direct interaction between NS-CDs and GSH was investigated. Under excitation at 370 nm, GSH hardly emits fluorescence and does not affect the fluorescence characteristics of NS-CDs (Fig. 3
a). Therefore, it is not feasible to directly detect GSH by utilizing the interaction between GSH and NS-CDs. Ag+ can react with GSH (Eq. (3)), but its product does not emit light under 370 nm excitation. However, Ag+ can oxidize OPD (Eq. (4)) to produce yellow fluorescence (OPDox: λex = 410 nm, λem = 560 nm). No fluorescence was observed when OPD was added to the reaction system of Ag+ and GSH. This indicates that GSH has prevented the generation of OPDox (Fig. 3 b). Next, we added Ag+ and OPD to the NS-CDs solution, and yellow fluorescence was observed as a result. This indicates the generation of OPDox, which masks the blue fluorescence of NS-CDs (Fig. 3 c). If Ag+, OPD, and GSH are added simultaneously to the NS-CDs solution, only blue fluorescence is observed due to the absence of OPDox in the system.
(3)
(4)

If the mass ratio of GSH to Ag+ is greater than 1:1 (stoichiometric ratio according to Eq. (3)), there is an excess of GSH in the system, and there is no excess Ag+ to oxidize OPD, so the system exhibits blue fluorescence; On the contrary, the system exhibits yellow fluorescence. The content of GSH determines the final fluorescence characteristics of the system when the content of Ag+ is controlled constant. Therefore, we have successfully constructed a ratio fluorescence sensing platform based on NS-CDs blue fluorescence and Ag+-assisted OPDox yellow fluorescence for detecting GSH. The absorption spectra corresponding to the above process are listed in Fig. S6. The absorption spectra of NS-CDs solution before and after the addition of GSH almost overlap (Fig. S6a), indicating that GSH does not affect the conjugated structure of NS-CDs, which is the reason why GSH does not affect the fluorescence characteristics of NS-CDs. OPD has obvious absorption peaks at 212 and 288 nm with a shoulder peak at 235 nm. In addition to these three peaks, OPDox also produces a shoulder peak at 260 nm and an absorption peak at 418 nm (Fig. S6b). This is the result of the increase in the planar rigid conjugated structure of OPDox, which is consistent with the strong fluorescence characteristics displayed by OPDox in Fig. 3 b. Compared to GSH, a 260 nm absorption peak appears in the absorption spectrum of Ag-GSH, indicating that the reaction between Ag+ and GSH generates a new substance (Fig. S6c). The absorption spectrum obtained by simple mixing of Ag+ and OPD is the same as that of the OPD itself. The absorption peak appearing at 418 nm indicates that a simple mixture of Ag+ and OPD can produce OPDox (Fig. S6d). In the mixed system of Ag-GSH and OPD, the absorption peak of Ag-GSH has a blue-shift from 260 to 250 nm, while the absorption peak of OPD at 288 nm is almost masked by the tail absorption of Ag-GSH, and there is no absorption peak at 418 nm (Fig. S6e). When GSH is insufficient in the system, excess Ag+ can oxidize OPD to generate OPDox, resulting in a 418 nm absorption peak. Adding NS-CDs to a mixture of Ag-GSH and OPD or Ag-GSH and OPDox hardly changes the absorption spectral characteristics of the mixture (Fig. S6f). Similarly, the addition of NS-CDs does not alter the absorption spectral characteristics of OPD or OPDox (Fig. S6g). The above absorption spectrum analysis results effectively confirm the interactions between various substances in the ratio fluorescence sensing platform constructed by NS-CDs and OPDox.

To further elucidate the mechanism of OPDox quenching the fluorescence of NS-CDs, the fluorescence spectra of NS-CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of OPDox were compared and analyzed (Fig. 3 d). When NS-CDs were excited at 390 nm, the maximum emission peak was observed at 450 nm. The absorption spectrum of OPDox overlapped with the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of NS-CDs to varying degrees. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of NS-CDs by OPDox may be caused by the IFE (Internal Filtration Effect) [34,35]. Fig. 3 e and f are fluorescence decay curves of NS-CDs and OPDox excited by 390 nm and emitted at 450 and 560 nm, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of NS-CDs at 450 nm was 3.96 ns. In the presence of OPDox, it was 3.90 ns. OPDox minimally effect the fluorescence lifetime of NS-CDs. The average fluorescence lifetime of OPDox was 1.96 ns, and in the presence of NS-CDs, it was 1.98 ns. Fluorescence lifetimes of materials are usually determined energy transfer process between donor and acceptor fluorophore. FRET (FÖrster Resonance Energy Transfer) influences the fluorescence lifetime of donor fluorophores, while IFE cannot [36]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of NS-CDs is caused by IFE, not FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition and composition optimization of sensor platform
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve GSH ratio detection, key factors were optimized. As shown in Fig. S7, the optimal excitation wavelength for NS-CDs is 370 nm, with an emission peak at 436 nm, exhibiting excitation dependence. The optimal excitation wavelength for OPDox is 410 nm, with an emission peak at 560 nm, showing excitation-independence. However, constructing ratio fluorescent probes requires the same excitation wavelength. And while ensuring the intensity of emission peaks, the distance between emission peaks should be increased as much as possible to broaden the range of detected color changes and improve sensitivity. Therefore, the optimal excitation wavelength for the ratio fluorescence probe was determined to be 390 nm, as the fluorescence intensity both of NS-CDs (λem = 450 nm) and OPDox (λem = 560 nm) is relatively strong at this excitation wavelength.

As shown in Fig. S8a, when the addition volume of OPD is 60 µL, the fluorescence intensity reaches ceiling. This is consistent with the stoichiometric ratio of Eq. (4), indicating that OPD reacts completely with Ag+ in the system. As shown in Fig. S8b, when the pH of the system is 7.5, the fluorescence intensity of OPDox is the highest at 560 nm. Furthermore, the effect of the incubation temperature of Ag+ and OPD on the fluorescence intensity of OPDox was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of OPDox reached a plateau at 40 °C, and a further increase in temperature did not affect the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S8c). Therefore, 40 °C was selected as the optimal incubation temperature. Under the optimal conditions determined above, the effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of OPDox was investigated, as shown in Fig. S8d. It is clear fluorescence signals are generated after incubation for 1 min. When the incubation time was extended to 10 min, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum value and did not change with the continuous extension. Therefore, the best incubation time was 10 min. Therefore, the optimal conditions for the fluorescence sensing platform were determined to be an excitation wavelength of 390 nm, pH 7.5, and incubation at 40 °C for 10 min.

When the amounts of Ag+ and OPD are fixed, the volume of NS-CDs directly affect the color sensitivity of the ratio fluorescent platform and the sensitivity of detecting GSH. Fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 4
a) was obtained by adding different volumes of NS-CDs under optimal conditions. With increasing NS-CDs volumes, the intensity of emission peak at 460 nm gradually increases, while the emission intensity of 560 nm (from OPDox) decreases. However, when the volume of NS-CDs reaches 60 μL, it no longer decreases. If different volumes of NS-CDs is added to the system containing Ag+, OPD, and GSH (100 μL, 6 mmol/L), the fluorescence spectrum is shown in Fig. 4 b. The addition of NS-CDs below 60 μL yields emission peaks at 560 nm from OPDox and 460 nm from NS-CDs. When more than 30 μL NS-CDs was added the intensity of the emission peak at 460 nm is higher than that of the 560 nm emission peak. Starting from the addition of 30 μL of NS-CDs, the intensity of the 460 nm emission peak is greater than that of the 560 nm emission peak. This is because GSH reacts with some Ag+ (equation (3), and the remaining Ag+ can still oxidize some OPD (equation (4), resulting in weak emission at 560 nm. Moreover, when the addition of NS-CDs is greater than or equal to 60 μL, the 560 nm emission peak further weakens and is covered by the tail of the 460 nm emission peak. Fig. 4 c shows the relationship between the intensity ratio of two fluorescence emission peaks IBlue/IYellow in the two systems as a function of the volume of NS-CDs. When the volume of NS-CDs added is 60 μL, the difference between the IBlue/IYellow of the NS-CDs + Ag+ + OPD + GSH system and the NS-CDs + Ag+ + OPD system is maximized. From the fluorescence image in Fig. 4 d, it can be observed that for the system without GSH, as the volume of NS-CDs increases, the probe gradually changes from yellow fluorescence to light yellow, white, and light blue fluorescence, while the system with GSH gradually changes from light yellow to white, light blue, and blue fluorescence. The appearance of white fluorescence is due to the complementary colors of blue and yellow that co-exist. Considering the more significant difference between yellow fluorescence and blue fluorescence, the volume of NS-CDs is controlled at 60 μL.

When the concentration of GSH increased from 500 to 2500 nmol/L, the fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 4 e. As the concentration of GSH increases, the yellow fluorescence intensity gradually decreases and the blue fluorescence intensity gradually strengthens. There is a linear relationship between IBlue/IYellow and GSH concentration (Fig. 4 f). The linear equation is:
(5)
I
Blue
/
I
Yellow
= 0.0015
C
GSH
- 0.4669
(
R
2
= 0.9435 )

According to formula:
(6)
The
d e t e c t i o n
l i m i t
L O D
= 3 σ / K
where σ is the standard deviation of signals from 10 blank parallel measurements, and K represents the slope of the linear equation. An LOD of 9.36 nmol/L is calculated. The illustration in Fig. 4 f is a fluorescence photo. When the concentration of GSH increases to 1000 nmol/L, it exhibits white fluorescence (indicating a balance between blue and yellow fluorescence). When the concentration of GSH increases to 2500 nmol/L, yellow fluorescence disappears and blue fluorescence is displayed. In addition, the change in fluorescence color can also be observed from the CIE color coordinates (Fig. 4 g). If GSH is used to quench the fluorescence of Ag+ and OPD systems, corresponding linear equations can also be established (Fig. S9):
(7)
I /
I 0
= - 0.0002
C
GSH
+ 0.9330
(
R
2
= 0.974 )
and the calculated LOD is 90.45 nmol/L. Compared to this, the detection limit of GSH by the ratio fluorescent probe containing NS-CDs was reduced by nearly 10 times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of sensor platform
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the influence of temperature on detection accuracy, we conducted experiments under room temperatures ranging from 20 °C to 45 °C. The results demonstrated excellent sensor stability in detecting GSH, indicating that temperature variations within this range have a negligible impact on the experimental results (Fig. S10).The fluorescence spectra obtained by adding metal cations, acid anions, or amino acids (Zn2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ac−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, Trp, Ala, Met, Pro, Phe, Ser, Val, UA, and Urea) to the sensing system composed of NS-CDs, Ag+, and OPD, are shown in Fig. S11a. It is obvious that the fluorescence spectra did not change after adding these substances, and only the addition of GSH caused a significant increase in blue fluorescence, indicating that the constructed sensing platform has good selectivity for GSH. When these substances were added to the sensing system together with GSH, at a concentration 20 times higher than GSH, the measured IBlue/IYellow was the same as when GSH was present alone (Fig. S11b). This indicates that the presence of these substances does not interfere with the determination of GSH by the sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual determination of GSH using a smartphone
ce:section.ce:para:
We have designed a 390 nm ultraviolet lamp integrated with a smartphone for on-site visualization detection of GSH based on the sensor platform and the results listed in Fig. 4 a. As shown in Fig. 5
a, with the increase of GSH concentration, significant color changes (from yellow to blue) can be observed under 390 nm ultraviolet irradiation. A series of photos were analyzed using the RGB mode of the smartphone application program. The B/R value shows a linear relationship with GSH concentration within the range of 500–2500 nmol/L (y = 0.0016x − 0.398, R2 = 0.973), and the calculated LOD is 9.36 nmol/L.

A cascaded logic circuit was constructed (Fig. 5 b) based on the correlation between the sensing platform and GSH. NS-CDs, Ag+ + OPD, and GSH serve as the three inputs of the logic gate, designated as Input 1, Input 2, and Input 3, respectively. The blue fluorescence emitted by NS-CDs is defined as Output 1, while the yellow fluorescence emitted by Ag+ + OPD is defined as Output 2. In Fig. 5 b1, blue fluorescence (Output 1) is observed only when Input 1 is present. When both 1 and 3 are inputted simultaneously, Output 1 remains, as GSH does not affect the fluorescence of NS-CDs. In Fig. 5 b2, yellow fluorescence (Output 2) is observed only when Input 2 is present. However, when both 2 and 3 are input simultaneously, no fluorescence is produced, indicating no output, due to the interaction between GSH and Ag+ blocking the production of yellow fluorescence.

For the ratio fluorescence sensor platform, NS-CDs and Ag+ + OPD are used as the two inputs for the first AND logic gate (Fig. 5 b3). In addition, the output of INHIBIT is either Output 1, Output 2, or Output 1 plus Output 2. The concentration of GSH determines the output result of the logic gate. The threshold for CGSH is set to 1000 nmol/L. If CGSH is greater than 1000 nmol/L, the sensing platform will display Output 1. At a CGSH of approximately 1000 nmol/L, the platform generates both Output 1 and Output 2 (blue and yellow fluorescence, complementary colors, ultimately displaying white). If CGSH is less than 1000 nmol/L, the sensing platform will display Output 2.

Fluorescence generation is defined as output “1”, while non-generation or fluorescence quenching is defined as “0”. As shown in Fig. 5 c, when only NS-CD is inputted or both NS-CD and GSH are inputted simultaneously, blue fluorescence is generated, so the outputs of FBlue and FYellow are both “1, 0”. When only OPDox (Ag+ + OPD) is present in the system, yellow fluorescence is generated, and the outputs of FBlue and FYellow are “0, 1”. If GSH is input simultaneously, the yellow fluorescence quenches or disappears, and the output is “0, 0”. When NS-CDs and OPDox (Ag+ + OPD) are inputted simultaneously, due to the internal filtering effect, the blue fluorescence of NS-CDs weakens and the system presents yellow fluorescence, resulting in an output of “0, 1”. After inputting GSH, the blue fluorescence enhances and the yellow fluorescence weakens or quenches, and the output result is converted to “1, 0”. In short, only when two sensing modules are input simultaneously and GSH is added, will the fluorescence color change. The corresponding truth table (Fig. 5 d) facilitates straightforward determination of GSH existence from the dual-mode output states of “0, 1” and “1, 0”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH in human urine

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from the inedible part of jackfruit, and the fluorescence quantum yield of NS-CDs was increased 1.93 times by introducing nitrogen and sulfur through N-acetyl-L-cysteine. A ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for sensitive detection of GSH was constructed, utilizing the blue fluorescence of NS-CDs and the yellow fluorescence of OPDox, a product of Ag+ oxidation of OPD. The detection limit was 9.36 nmol/L. Based on this sensing platform, an RGB detection method using smartphones was established, along with a dual-channel molecular logic gate. The GSH content in human urine was successfully determined using the dual fluorescence emission signal ratio (IBlue/IYellow) and the color change detected by smartphone RGB analysis. This result not only conforms to the inference derived from logic gates, but also highly consistent with the results obtained by standard HPLC methods. This further confirms the accuracy and applicability of the sensing platform. The combination of smartphone color recognition applications and logic gates enable the conversion of color changes distinguishable by the naked eye into specific values. This innovative strategy not only utilizes waste materials but also provides a portable, visual, and accurate method for GSH detection.

Kamile Arkin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuxin Zheng: Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Xuesong Ma: Software, Data curation. Yuting Xu: Validation, Data curation. Xue Jiang: Validation, Data curation. Qingkun Shang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22202170, 21872022, and 21573039).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112567.

ce:section.ce:para:
